message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Outpatient physicians need guidance to support their clinical decisions regarding management of patients with COVID-19, specifically whether to hospitalize a patient or if managed as an outpatient, how closely to follow them.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To develop and prospectively validate a clinical prediction rule to predict the likelihood of hospitalization for outpatients with COVID-19 that does not require laboratory testing or imaging, including during the current Omicron wave.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Derivation and temporal validation of a clinical prediction rule, and prospective validation of two externally derived clinical prediction rules.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Primary and urgent care clinics in a Pennsylvania health system.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Patients 12 years and older presenting to outpatient clinics who had a positive polymerase chain reaction test for COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main outcomes and measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Classification accuracy (percentage in each risk group hospitalized) and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Overall, 4.0% of outpatients in the early derivation cohort (5843 patients presenting before 3/1/21), 4.2% in the late validation cohort (3806 patients presenting 3/1/21 to 9/30/21), and 1.9% in an Omicron cohort were ultimately hospitalized. We developed and temporally validated four simple risk scores. The base score included age, dyspnea, and the presence of a comorbidity, with the other scores adding fever, respiratory rate and/or oxygen saturation. All had very good overall accuracy (AUC 0.85-0.87) and classified at least half of patients into a low risk with a < 1% likelihood of hospitalization. Hospitalization rates in the Omicron cohort were 0.22%, 1.3% and 8.7% for the base score. Two externally derived risk scores identified more low risk patients, but with a higher overall risk of hospitalization than our novel risk scores.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions and relevance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    A simple risk score applicable to outpatient and telehealth settings can classify over half of COVID-19 outpatients into a very low risk group with a 0.22% hospitalization risk in the Omicron cohort. The Lehigh Outpatient COVID Hospitalization (LOCH) risk score is available online as a free app:
                    <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="uri" xlink:href="https://ebell-projects.shinyapps.io/LehighRiskScore/">https://ebell-projects.shinyapps.io/LehighRiskScore/</jats:ext-link>
                    .
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key points</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Question</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Is it possible to predict the eventual likelihood of hospitalization for outpatients with COVID-19 using simple non-laboratory based risk scores?</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Findings</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>We created and temporally validated in the same population 4 risk scores with 3 to 5 predictors that do not require laboratory testing. Groups with low (0.34% to 0.89%), moderate (4.0% to 6.2%), and high-risk (19.2% to 25.2%) of hospitalization were identified. The risk scores were also accurate in an Omicron dominant cohort with hospitalization rates of 0.22% to 0.43% in the low-risk groups, 1.3% to 1.7% in the moderate risk groups, and 8.7% to 15.3% in the high risk groups.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Meaning</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Simple risk scores can help support decisions about hospitalization in the outpatient setting.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: Simple risk scores to predict hospitalization or death in outpatients with COVID-19 including the Omicron variant

reference.unstructured: U.S. Census Bureau QuickFacts: Allentown city, Pennsylvania. Accessed January 10, 2022. https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/allentowncitypennsylvania
